coredata.dc:title: Ag-LaFeO3/NCQDs p-n heterojunctions for superior methanol gas sensing performance
coredata.dc:creator: Rong, Qian
Zhang, Yumin
Li, Kejin
Wang, Huapeng
Hu, Jicu
Zhu, Zhongqi
Zhang, Jin
Liu, Qingju
coredata.dc:description: The unique electronic properties (electron mobility and carrier lifetime) are key factors for metal oxide semiconductor devices that directly determine the device's performance and application. Electron mobility of metal oxide semiconductor has a certain impact on response and operating temperature of gas sensor. Heterojunction composites of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and perovskite materials have attracted much attention due to their great potential for gas sensors. In this paper, a gas sensor for monitoring methanol gas was developed based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/Ag-LaFeO3 (NCQDs/Ag-LaFeO3) p-n heterojunction materials (NALPN). NCQDS and Ag-LaFeO3 form a heterojunction, which improves the carrier transport ability and separation of electron-hole pairs. The surface of NCQDs contains COOH, optimize the selectivity of Ag-LaFeO3 to methanol gas. Gas sensitivity test results show that the NALPN to 5 ppm methanol gas possessed high response (73), low operating temperature (92 °C) and good selectivity. These excellent properties prove that NALPN materials are the main competitors for future gas sensitive materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NALPN
P-N heterojunction
Methanol
Gas sensor
Operating temperature

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of microwave-assisted synthesis of NALPN and the fabricated gas sensor.
FT-IR spectrum of Ag-LaFeO3 cross-linker, ALPP, NALPN and MAA.

(a) Weight ratio of different elements in NALPN, (b) EDS spectra of 1.00% NALPN; (c) SEM image of 1.00% NALPN, EDS mapping images for the sample: (d) overlay, (e) La, (f) Fe, (g) O, (h) Ag, (i) C and (j) N of 1.00% NALPN.

(a) TEM images of ALPP, (b) high resolution crystal lattice of ALPP, (c) TEM images of NALPN and (d) high resolution crystal lattice of NALPN, (e) TEM images of CQDs, (f) HRTEM images of CQD, (g) TEM images of NCQDs, (f) HRTEM images of NCQD.
Raman spectrum of (a) ALPP and (b) NALPN.
Isotherms of (a) ALPP and (b) NALPN and the inset are the pore size distribution curve. I–V characteristic curve of the (c) ALPP and (d) NALPN measured at room temperature in the dark.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of the ALPP (b) high magnification XPS spectra of O 1s for ALPP, (c) XPS survey spectrum of the NALPN and (d) high magnification XPS spectra of O 1s for NALPN.

(a) The resistances of the 0.25% ALPP sensors at the different operating temperature for air and 5 ppm methanol gas, (b) 1.00% NALPN sensor's resistance at the different operating temperature for air and 5 ppm methanol gas, response curves with different amount of doping of (c) ALPP (0.10%–1.00%) for 5 ppm methanol, response curves with different amount of doping of (d) NALPN (0.25%–1.25%) for 5 ppm methanol, response of 5 ppm different test gas of (e) 0.25% ALPP and response of 5 ppm different test gas of (f) 1.00%NALPN.

The response linearly increases with methanol concentration based on (a) 0.25% ALPP, (b) 1.00% NALPN, dynamic response curve of the (c) 0.25% ALPP, (d) 1.00% NALPN based gas sensors to methanol ranging from 10 to 500 ppm at 125 °C and 92 °C, respectively.
The response of 0.25% ALPP and 1.00% NALPN sensor toward 5 ppm methanol gases tested for 35 days at 125°C and 92°C, respectively.
A Mechanism of NALPN sensor for methanol gas: (a) in air, (b) in methanol.
Comparison of gas sensing properties based methanol sensors with different materials.

head.ce:title: Ag-LaFeO3/NCQDs p-n heterojunctions for superior methanol gas sensing performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Astronomy, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yunnan University, 650091, Kunming, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Colorado, Boulder, CO, 80309, United States

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The unique electronic properties (electron mobility and carrier lifetime) are key factors for metal oxide semiconductor devices that directly determine the device's performance and application. Electron mobility of metal oxide semiconductor has a certain impact on response and operating temperature of gas sensor. Heterojunction composites of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and perovskite materials have attracted much attention due to their great potential for gas sensors. In this paper, a gas sensor for monitoring methanol gas was developed based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/Ag-LaFeO3 (NCQDs/Ag-LaFeO3) p-n heterojunction materials (NALPN). NCQDS and Ag-LaFeO3 form a heterojunction, which improves the carrier transport ability and separation of electron-hole pairs. The surface of NCQDs contains COOH, optimize the selectivity of Ag-LaFeO3 to methanol gas. Gas sensitivity test results show that the NALPN to 5 ppm methanol gas possessed high response (73), low operating temperature (92 °C) and good selectivity. These excellent properties prove that NALPN materials are the main competitors for future gas sensitive materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Methanol is widely used in many products, such as pigments, pharmaceuticals and chemical products etc [1]. However, it is toxic and the is main reason of human nerve poisoning and cardiovascular diseases. Nowadays, there are several methods to detect methanol gas, such as chromatography [2], spectrophotometric method [3], electrochemical method [4], catalytic luminescence [5] and gas sensor method [6], etc. The first four methods require expensive instruments, so they are high cost and hard to widely apply. Gas sensor is considered to be an effective method for the detection of methanol gas because of the advantages of high response, simple operation, low cost and small device.

The principle of a metal oxide semiconductor gas sensor is the conductivity changes caused by the reaction of gas molecules at the surface of the material, so the material can respond to the target gas. At present, Liu et al. demonstrated that SnO2 hollow spheres exhibit excellent sensing performance with a response of 10 for 10 ppm methanol gas at 225 °C [7]. Vijay et al. prepared mesoporous Ag-doped TiO2/SnO2 nano-composite by a hydrothermal method, which exhibit a response of 15 to 10 ppm methanol gas at 275 °C [8]. Tang et al. reported a SnO2-ZnO2 prepared by a step wise-heating electro spinning method with a response of 7–10 ppm methanol gas at 250 °C [9]. However, these gas sensing properties of methanol gas sensors are still unsatisfactory, such as high operating temperature (more than 220 °C) and lower response (<15). The operating temperature and response are related to the conductivity and electron mobility of the gas sensing materials. Compared with other gas sensing materials, Ag-LaFeO3 is more potential [10–12]. To further enhance the response and reduce the operating temperature of Ag-LaFeO3 to methanol gas, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were introduced.

CQDs are composed of single or several layers of graphite, the size of which is within 3–20 nm in three dimensions [13]. It is considered as a new nanomaterial and applied in biological imaging [14], sensors [15] and photoelectric element [16] and other fields. Because CQDs have abundant functional groups, it has good water solubility and functionalization [17]. Therefore, there are abundant adsorption sites on the surfaces of CODs.

In this paper, in order to obtain a methanol gas sensor with high response, good selectivity and low operating temperature, the p-n heterojunction between nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot (NCQDs) and Ag-LaFeO3 was mainly studied. Ag-LaFeO3 is a p-type semiconductor, to increase the carrier mobility at lower the operating temperature, n-type NCQDs are selected to form a p-n heterojunction with Ag-LaFeO3. In order to study the role of NCQDs, different amount of NCQDs loading 0.10%, 0.25%, 0.50%, 0.75%, 1.00% and 1.25% were composited with Ag-LaFeO3. The NCQDs/Ag-LaFeO3 heterojunction (NALPN) shows effective charge separation. The carboxyl functional group on the surface of NCQDs has the specific recognition ability for methanol gas and improves the selectivity of NALPN to methanol gas. Therefore, NALPN possesses good gas sensitive response and selectivity for methanol gas compared to CQDs/Ag-LaFeO3 (ALPP) and Ag-LaFeO3, this encouraging result can strongly support the importance of semiconductor NALPN heterojunctions in improving gas sensing performance.

The synthesis method is described as follows: citric acid, lanthanum nitrate, iron nitrate, silver nitrate, polyethylene glycol 2000 (PEG 2000) as well as deionized water were mixed stirring at 90 °C for 10 h. Then put it into the microwave synthesizing device (CEM) for 4 h at 75 °C as solution A. Methanol mixed with methacylic acid (MAA) was treated for 30 min by magnetic stirring, and stand to form solution B. Then, Azo diisobutyl cyanide (AIBN) mixed with methanol, solution B and solution A. The final solution was treated by magnetic stirred at 60 °C for 10 h with water bath then dried. Finally, the yellow powder of Ag-LaFeO3 was obtained after annealing at 800 °C for 2 h in an oven.

Preparation of CQDs: 500 mg graphite powder was dispersed in 80 ml N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP). It was dispersed in the ultrasonic cleaning instrument (100 W, 53 kHz) for 10 h. A yellow liquid contains dispersed CQDs and some precipitate was obtained. Removal of precipitates by centrifugation at 4500 rpm for 45 min to obtain a uniformly dispersed black solution. NMP was removed by vacuum evaporation at 100 °C to obtain CQDs.

Preparation of NCQDs: 1 g of graphite powder was added into 100 ml HNO3 and ultra-sonicated for 5 min. Then, the mixed suspension was refluxed for 6 h at 180 °C and subsequently added into 30 ml of deionized water. The graphite powder and the HNO3 were separated through filtration with a microporous membrane (pore size 0.22 mm), and dried at 60 °C in air, 500 mg N doping graphite powder were dispersed in 80 ml NMP as following. The decentralized treatment was under in the ultrasonic cleaning instrument for 10 h. A yellow liquid contains dispersed NCQDs and some precipitate was obtained. Removal of precipitates by centrifugation at 4500 rpm for 45 min obtain a uniformly dispersed black solution. NMP was removed by vacuum evaporation at temperature of 100 °C to obtain NCQDs.

After Ag-LaFeO3 was mixed with CQDs and NCQDs according different weight ratio (0.10%, 0.25%, 0.50%, 0.75%, 1.00% and 1.25%), the mixtures were added into 50 ml deionized water, respectively. The ALPP and NALPN were prepared via microwave-assisted synthesis, the detailed preparation process was shown in Fig. 1 .

Mix the appropriate amount of prepared ALPP and NALPN with methanol in order to take shape a mushy solution, which applied on the ceramic tube to form a thick film. The four platinum wires are distributed at both ends of the ceramic tube. The ceramic tube ages 5 V for 180 h in air. The structure of the gas sensor was displayed in the Fig. 1. The performance of the gas sensor is tested through a static method, which was detailed test mechanism of the sensors sees ref. [18].

The crystal structure of the prepared ALPP and NALPN were characterized though X-ray diffraction (XRD) by Japan AXS D/MAX-3BX advance device handled at 25 mA and 35 kV with Cu Kα scattered (λ = 1.5406 Å) in the 2θ range of 10°-90°. The functional group was identified by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, FTS-40). The detailed morphology features and crystallization conditions of the material were surveyed via field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, S-3400 N) as well as transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100, Hitachi, Japan). Raman scattering was performed on a Nano-finder (Tokyo Instruments Co, Osaka, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to test the valence state of the ALPP and NALPN using a K-Alpha+ spectroscopy at 6.7 × 10−8 Pa. The XPS were charge calibrated with respect to the adventitious C 1s (284.8 eV). The BET surface area (SBET) of material was measure from Quadrasorb-evo instrument based on the nitrogen adsorption isotherm.

Gas sensing response of ALPP and NALPN with 0.25% and 1.00% (0.25% and 1.00% are the weight ratio of CQDs and NCQDs to Ag-LaFeO3 respectively) is better than those with other weight ratios (0.10%, 0.50%, 0.75% and 1.25%). This paper mainly discusses 0.25% ALPP and 1.00% NALPN.

XRD of the ALPP and NALPN are showed in Fig. 2 . These peaks displayed that the structure of ALPP and the NALPN is orthogonal perovskite. The main peak appearing in the figure are ascribed to the diffraction of (101), (121), (220), (202), (240), (242) and (204) crystal planes, which is perfectly matches with the standard JCPDS card no. 37-1493 [19]. No precursor residue had been detected indicating that the samples are with high purity.

Fig. 3 shows the FT-IR spectroscopy of Ag-LaFeO3 cross-linker, ALPP, NALPN and MAA. The wide absorption band at 3420–3600 cm−1 of all the samples are related to the telescopic vibration of the OH group. In the curve of Ag-LaFeO3 cross-linker, the absorption peaks at ˜565 cm−1 and ˜1632 cm−1 are associated with the flexural vibration of Fe-O and La-O ( Fig. 3(a)), respectively. In the curve of MAA, the peaks around ∼1210 cm−1 and ˜1709 cm−1 are the vibration of CO and CO in carboxylic acid, respectively (Fig. 3(d)). In the curve of ALPP and NALPN, the bands at ˜1505 cm−1 are attributed to the vibration of carbonyl in COOH, the disappearance of the peaks at ˜1709 cm−1 compared with MAA curve suggested the effect between Ag-LaFeO3 and MAA (Fig. 3(b) and (c)). The mutual effect attributed to the synergy between functional groups in MAA and La in ALPP and NALPN, it is proved that the metal carbonyl complex is formed [20].

The distribution of La, Fe, Ag, O, N and C elements in NALPN is analysed via EDS spectroscopy and EDS mapping analysis, which are shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4(a) shows the error bar of the weight ratios of different elements obtained by the EDS spectroscopy of the 0.25%, 0.50%, 0.75%, 1.00% and 1.25% NALPN. Fig. 4(b) is the EDS spectrum of NALPN, which is a strong evidence for the presence La, Fe, O, C and a small amount of Ag and N. Fig. 4(c) and (d) is the SEM and mapping images of the elements of the NALPN power sample, we observe that the NCQDs are uniformly mixed with Ag-LaFeO3, and further confirmed the elements distribution of La, Fe, Ag, O, N and C in the 1.00% NALPN. Fig. 4(e–j) shows the mapping results for a single element, this indicates the successful introduction of NCQDs nanoparticles in NALPN.

TEM and HRTEM give insight into morphology and nanoparticle size of the as-prepared ALPP, NALPN, CQDs and NCQDs. For ALPP (Fig. 5 (a)), the particles are irregular and aggregated with sizes range from 49 to 95 nm. The larger particles are made up of many small grains. Fig. 5(b) shows the HRTEM image of ALPP particles which exhibit the internal microstructure information. Fig. 5(c) exhibits that the particle size of NALPN is relatively uniform and without aggregated into blocks. The particle size of NALPN is from 30 to 125 nm. HRTEM images of the ALPP and NALPN are given in Fig. 5(b) and (d), and we can clearly see the lattice stripes that indicate good crystallinity. The corresponding insets show the fringe spacing is 0.40 nm and 0.32 nm of ALPP and NALPN, respectively. The grain sizes of CQDs and NCQDs are below 10 nm, as shown in Fig. 5(e–h), and the crystallization of CQDs and NCQDs is very good. Raman spectra is an important tool for characterizing the carbon materials with G and D bands [21], and the quality of the crystalline samples and structure of multi-oxide system [22]. Fig. 6 exhibits the Raman spectrum of the ALPP and NALPN. The 2D band consists of a single Lorentzian [23,24]. In addition, a D band is observed, which attributed to the defective graphite edges formed during the element doping [25].

As shown in Fig. 6, Raman spectra display two G bands, the large peak redshift (Δν =25 cm−1, NCQDs) indicates that N can stiffen the Raman scattering frequencies of G and 2D peaks of CQDs, resulting in the n-type doping [26,27]. Because of the doping process, oxygen containing groups such as OH, COOH and specific functional groups (-N) are introduced on the edge and the base surface, so the CQDs have good solubility in water and other solvents. The peaks at 299 and 456 cm−1 corresponds to Ag and B3g distribution pattern. The peak at 629 cm−1 is due to two photons and the scattering related to the impurity. These peaks are consistent with the reported LaFeO3 [28].

The SBET of the ALPP and NALPN have a great affect on the performance of gas sensor. The SBET and distribution curves of pore-size of two materials are determined by using N2 adsorption and desorption isotherm (as shown in Fig. 7 (a,b)). The SBET and pore-size of ALPP and NALPN are respectively 11.39 m2 g−1 and 11.18 m2 g−1 about 30–32 nm and 30–46 nm. It can be seen that the ALPP and NALPN have larger SBET and more pores compared with pure Ag-LaFeO3. Thus the ALPP and NALPN will have better gas sensitive response [28]. Fig. 7(c) and (d) are the typical current-voltage (I–V) characteristics of the ALPP and NALPN films in the dark. It can be seen from the curve that the p-type Ag-LaFeO3 film has Schottky contact with the CQDs and NCQDs. This shows that a perfect heterojunction is formed between Ag-LaFeO3 and NCQDs.

The XPS spectra of ALPP and NALPN were determined to analyze electronic states and surface chemical composition. As seen in Fig. 8 , the XPS survey spectrum confirms the presence of Ag (4p), La (3d, 4p, 4d), Fe (2p), O (1 s), N (1 s) and C (1 s) for the ALPP and NALPN. The XPS show a C1 s at 284.8 eV and O1 s at 531 eV, which further confirm Raman spectroscopy deduction. The XPS La 3d and Fe 2p energy level display that the lanthanum ions and iron ions exist in the form of +3 valence [29]. In the case of NALPN, the intensity of N1s attributed to the N doping [30]. The O 1s XPS patterns of ALPP and NALPN (Fig. 8(b) and (d)) are wide as well as asymmetrical. The O 1s XPS patterns fit two chemical conditions by Gauss's rule [31], one is Olatt at 528.2 eV, the other is OH at 530.5 eV, where Olatt is attributed to lattice oxygen and OH ascribed to the absorbed Ox − (O− and O2 −). Because the cohesive energies of O− or OH− ions can be 2.1–2.5 eV higher than that of lattice oxygen, So the lattice oxygen could not react with the gas and unable to affect the formation of main charge-carrier holes in materials. The Ox − ions absorbed by the material will interact with the gas and increase the holes’ density [32], the response of the material to gases is further improved.

As we know, the response of gas sensor is the change in relative resistance in air and test gas. The resistance of the resultant 0.25% ALPP and 1.00% NALPN sensors changes with operating temperature in air and methanol gas are shown in Fig. 9 (a) and (b). Fig. 9(c) and (d) show the response of ALPP (0.10%–1.00%) and NALPN (0.25%–1.25%) sensors to 5 ppm methanol gases. The sensors with 0.25% ALPP and 1.00% NALPN exhibit a highest response for methanol gas from 50 °C to 200 °C compared to other ratios. To investigate the selectivity of 0.25% ALPP and 1.00% NALPN sensor, the response for 5 ppm different gases (including formaldehyde, methanol, toluene, acetone and gasoline) were analyzed as shown Fig. 9(e, f). It can be found that the two sensors behave well in selectivity to methanol gas. It can be seen that 0.25% ALPP and 1.00% NALPN exhibit higher response (60, 73) to methanol gas than the other test gases (≤12, 5) at 125 °C and 92 °C, respectively. The results show that the introducing of CQDs and NCQDs in Ag-LaFeO3 improves the gas sensing performance of methanol gas. Compared with the recent reported as shown in Table 1 [33–43], this work possesses better methanol sensing properties. The selectivity of 1.00% NALPN is better than that of 0.25% ALPP for methanol gas, the reason can be attributed to, first, the selectivity of Ag-LaFeO3 is improved with molecular imprinting technology, as illustrated by Fig. 1, when the template molecule (methanol) is mixed with functional monomers (methacrylic acid (MAA)), methanol interacts with MAA via hydrogen bonding to form a complex. The obtained complex is then copolymerized with a large amount of cross-linker (Ag-LaFeO3). Finally, after removing the template molecule, a recognition cavity complementary to the methanol molecule is formed on the surface of Ag-LaFeO3, which has many recognition sites, high recognition and binding ability for methanol and improves the selectivity of the NALPN sensor. Second, the surface of NCQDs contains COOH, which can further improve the selectivity of 1.00% NALPN to methanol gas. The 1.00% NALPN has better response to methanol gas than Ag-LaFeO3, because ecause NCQD bridge Ag-LaFeO3 grain boundaries which play the role of electron transport and form the shortened transport paths, which improves the conductivity and charge transfer rate of Ag-LaFeO3 [44]. Considering the performance and cost of the sensor, the 0.25% ALPP and 1.00% NALPN methanol gas sensor is the best sensor for the next step of research.

It can be found in Fig. 10 (a) and (b) that the response of the sensor linearly increased with the methanol concentration from 10 to 500 ppm, indicating that the sensor can be used as real-time detection for methanol gas. Furthermore, the response and recovery times of 0.25% ALPP and 1.00% NALPN are 20 s and 19 s, 50.9 s and 54.3 s, respectively. Fig. 10(c) and (d) presents a dynamic response of the sensor based on 0.25% ALPP and 1.00% NALPN operated at 125 °C and 92 °C under a methanol gas concentration in the range of 10 ppm to 500 ppm, respectively. Obviously, the response value dramatically increased with the increase of methanol concentration before 300 ppm, when the methanol concentration is higher than 300 ppm, the rising trend becomes extremely slow.

The stability of the device is a key factor in practical applications. The stability to 5 ppm methanol gas of 0.25% ALPP and 1.00% NALPN was tested respectively at 125 °C and 92 °C for 35 days as seen in Fig. 11 . The response of the sensor to methanol gas has a certain fluctuation with small amplitude. Therefore, the sensor based on 0.25% ALPP and 1.00% NALPN have good stability to methanol gas.

The principle of the ALPN sensor is according to the surface resistance control process just as Fig. 12 shows. During the test process, the sensor was exposed to air at first, oxygen adsorbed on the surface of ALPN combining with free electrons trapped on ALPN surface to form O2
− , O − , O2- and other oxygen ions [45]. This process will form a thin space charge layer and relatively low barriers to form a lower resistance state (Ra). When the material is in the methanol (CH3OH), unlike NO2 gas (electron withdrawing) [46], methanol is used as electronic donor, so the methanol gas reacts with the adsorbed oxygen forming CO2 and H2O and releasing the electrons [47]. The space charge layer on the ALPN surface becomes thick and the sensor resistance (Rg) increases. Therefore, the response of ALPN is obtained according to the S = Rg/Ra formula. The NALPN sensors have good response compared to pure Ag-LaFeO3, resulting from the four factors as follows: firstly, the formation of p-n heterojunction between Ag-LaFeO3 and NCQDs plays an important role in the enhanced response of NALPN to methanol gas. When Ag-LaFeO3 is in contact with NCQDs, electrons are transferred from the conduction band of NCQDs to the conduction band of Ag-LaFeO3 until the Fermi level is reached. The band gaps of NCQDs and Ag-LaFeO3 are well matched [11,48], which is advantageous for separating electron hole pairs and improving carrier transport capacity. Secondly, the surface of NCQDs contains COOH, which can improve the selectivity of 1.00% NALPN to methanol gas. Because of the good conductivity of NCQDs, the conductivity of NALPN are increased by introducing NCQDs, thus accelerating the diffusion of electrons and reducing the response and recovery time of materials to methanol gas [49]. Thirdly, the larger SBET of NALPN is beneficial to the adsorption of oxygen molecules on the surface of the material. Finally, NCQDs can produce Schottky contact at the intersection of NCQDs and Ag-LaFeO3 leading to a synergistic effect between Ag-LaFeO3 and NCQDs, the surface of NALPN is the Schottky barrier of forward bias, this helps to capture or migrate electrons from NCQDs nanoparticles to Ag-LaFeO3. Thus, the response of NALPN to methanol gas is enhanced by the incorporation of NCQDs.

In summary, ALPP and NALPN were synthesized via sol-gel assisted microwave chemical synthesis. The results show that the addition of CQDs and NCQDs significantly improves the gas sensing response of ALPP and NALPN to methanol gas. The response of the 0.25% ALPP and 1.00% NALPN sensor to 5 ppm methanol gas are 60 at 125°C and 73 at 92°C, respectively. The response and recovery times for 5 ppm methanol gas are 20 s and 19 s, 50.9 s and 54.3 s, respectively. The results show that the NALPN possessed high response, low operating temperature, good stability and selectivity to methanol gas. These excellent properties prove that the NALPN sensors are a potential candidate for measuring the concentration of methanol gas.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 51562038), the key Project of Natural Science Foundation of Yunnan (2018FY001(-011)) and Yunnan basic applied research project (no. 2017FB086).
